Mr. Vance, the final draft is ready. If your team approves the terms and conditions, we can sign today.
We approve, but we need one safeguard. If the company defaults, what happens?
If there is a default, you will get a thirty-day cure period, confirmed in writing.
Good. And unless that cure period is met, is the agreement still enforceable?
Yes. The contract stays fully binding. Unless both sides waive a clause, every term applies.
Understood. If anyone breaks a term, that is a breach, correct?
Exactly. So if we both sign now, we will execute the contract today.
Then let us sign. The deal is done.
If your team approves, we will sign today.
Once signed, the contract will be binding.
If the company defaults, the lender will act.
Unless we confirm in writing, the change will not count.
If both parties execute it, the contract will be enforceable.

Thank you for sending the final draft. Our legal team has reviewed the terms and conditions, and they are clear. We have one note: if the company defaults on a payment, we will need a thirty-day cure period in writing before any action. If you accept that, the agreement will be binding and enforceable. Once both parties sign, we will execute the contract this Friday.

Hi, this is a quick update on the closing. Good news -- the creditors have approved the final terms. If they sign by Thursday, we will execute the contract on Friday. Unless something changes, the deal will be fully binding next week. One small point remains: they want the cure period confirmed in writing. I think we will close this without any further sticking points. Let us talk before the signing.

Both parties agree on the final terms and conditions. The lawyers prepare a binding draft for review. Each side confirms any changes in writing. The signatories execute the contract. The agreement becomes enforceable on the closing date.
